title: Displaced Gaussian Boson Sampling for enhanced max-clique search
authors: Ewan Mer, Zhenghao Li, Shang Yu, Ian A. Walmsley, Raj B. Patel
abstract: Gaussian Boson Sampling (GBS) is capable of solving certain classes of graph problems owing to the samples produced by such a device having a connection to the hafnian matrix function.   In particular, a GBS device has been shown to provide an enhancement in the search of cliques -- or complete subgraphs -- in undirected weighted graphs over classical algorithms.   A graph can be mapped to a GBS experiment by configuring the squeezing parameters of the input states and programming the linear optical network.   In practice, limited squeezing and photon loss degrade the performance of the GBS device for max-clique search.   In comparison, coherent states -- often considered a classical resource due to their Poissonian statistics -- can be readily prepared across many modes using an attenuated laser.   In this paper, we report an enhancement of the success rate of GBS in finding maximum weighted cliques by adding displacements under lossy conditions or when a limited amount of squeezing is available.   Moreover, we report that this enhancement can be scaled up to large graphs with limited resource overheads.
summary: The paper proposes Displaced Gaussian Boson Sampling (D-GBS) as a way to improve the efficiency of finding maximum cliques in graphs. By adding coherent state displacements to the GBS process, the researchers show that the success rate can be significantly boosted even in the presence of high photon loss or low squeezing. This method scales effectively to larger graphs with minimal additional resource overhead.
main_problem: Enhancing the success rate of Gaussian Boson Sampling (GBS) for finding maximum-weighted cliques in undirected graphs, specifically overcoming performance degradation caused by photon loss and limited squeezing.
main_result: The introduction of Displaced Gaussian Boson Sampling (D-GBS) boosts the sampling probability of maximum-weighted cliques under high loss or low squeezing, providing a scalable enhancement with low resource overhead.
method: The authors use numerical simulations (Strawberry Fields) to compare D-GBS against GBS and classical algorithms, employing a classical post-processing routine of greedy-shrinking and local search on photon click patterns.
model_or_system: A photonic hardware platform utilizing Gaussian Boson Sampling (GBS) and its extension, Displaced Gaussian Boson Sampling (D-GBS), which incorporates single-mode squeezed vacuum states, a Haar-random linear optical network, and coherent state displacements.
key_observables: Photon number vectors, photon click patterns, and the success rate of identifying the maximum clique.
important_parameters: Squeezing parameters (r), displacement vectors (gamma), photon loss (eta), and the collision-free regime (M >> N^2).
main_assumptions: Assumes Haar-random unitaries, the anti-concentration property, and the collision-free regime; requires displacement to be a monotonic increasing function of node weight to avoid bias.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the D-GBS workflow; Figure 2 shows max-clique probability vs. gamma and squeezing; Figure 3 shows improvement across graph sizes; Figure 4 compares success rates against classical samplers; Figure 5 shows loss resilience; Figure 6 shows the effectiveness of local search; Figure 7 shows scaling of enhancement and resource ratios; Figure 8 shows probability distribution histograms.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of max-clique search in GBS, defines the D-GBS mathematical framework (loop hafnian), presents numerical results demonstrating enhancement under loss and low squeezing, analyzes scalability and complexity, and concludes with a comparison of resource overheads.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates how a specific type of non-classical resource (displacement) can be used to mitigate the detrimental effects of photon loss and limited squeezing in a computational task.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Displaced Gaussian Boson Sampling for max-clique search. A weighted graph G = (V, E) is mapped to a set of (r, γ, U) where r and γ are the vectors of squeezing param- eters and displacements, respectively, and U represents the passive unitary operation. Photons are detected with click- detectors at each output mode. Each photon click pattern is considered a subgraph S ⊆G and is fed into a greedy shrink- ing/local search algorithm.
FIG. 2. Left panel: Probability of measuring the max-clique of a graph as a function of γ and c. Right panel: Adjacency matrix of the graph.

FIG. 3. a) and b) Adjacency matrices. c) and d): Probability of measuring the max-clique of a graph as a function of γ and λmax = tanh rmax. e) and f): Maximal improvement of D-GBS with respect to GBS as a function of λmax by finding the optimal γ. a), c) and e): 8-node clique embedded in a 20-node Erd˝os-R´enyi graph with an edge probability of 0.2. b), d) and f): 14-node clique embedded in a 20-node Erd˝os- R´enyi graph with an edge probability of 0.2.

FIG. 4. Success rate of finding a max-clique in the TaceAs [17] by using a D-GBS sampler (green) with a total displacement of |αtot|2 = 10 and total squeezing | sinh rtot|2 = 2.34, a GBS sampler (dashed line) with | sinh rtot|2 = 2.34, Oh’s sampler (blue) [34] and a uniform sampler (red) after running a classical post-processing routine that consists of 7 iterations of local search after greedy shrinking with 500 sam- ples.

FIG. 5. Probability of finding the max-clique as a func- tion of loss for different loop strengths γ. A low level of displacement like γ = 0.2 can be used to enhance the probabil- ity of the max-clique by increasing the probability of sampling the max-clique. For an arbitrary level of loss, the displace- ment can be tuned to maximize the sampling probability and outperform the ideal GBS case.
